text: The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to lift the ureter cranially, placing the renal hilum on stretch for optimal dissection. The renal hilum is identified by tracing the left gonadal vein cranially to its insertion on the left renal vein, facilitating two-handed dissection of the hilar vessels.

The surgeon uses the non-dominant instrument to spread and develop windows of tissue, cauterizing vessels within these packets to expose the renal hilum. The dissection progresses in a bottom-up approach, revealing the lumbar vein off the left renal vein. The renal artery is located just below the junction of the gonadal vein and the renal vein, with careful dissection of small vessels aiding in its exposure.

The surgeon ligates renal vein tributaries, including the left gonadal vein and posterior lumbar vein, to enhance visualization of the renal artery situated posterior to the renal vein. The renal artery is identified and dissected, with spaces developed on either side of the vessels for future hilar clamping. The renal hilum is thoroughly dissected to ensure all branches of the renal artery are visible before applying bulldog occlusion.
